title: English–Latin & English–Greek Translator
description: Built an LSTM-based sequence-to-sequence neural machine translation system capable of translating English to Latin and English to Ancient Greek, trained on parallel corpora of classical texts.
story: In high school, I was obsessed with classical languages and frustrated that no good translation tools existed for Latin or Ancient Greek. So I built my own. Training LSTMs on parallel corpora of classical texts taught me the fundamentals of sequence-to-sequence modeling, attention mechanisms, and the pain of vanishing gradients. It was my first real encounter with deep learning — messy, humbling, and absolutely thrilling.

title: Products of Values of Polynomials in Finite Fields
description: Investigated products of values of polynomials in finite fields at the Research Science Institute, applying combinatorial and algebraic techniques to derive new theorems in number theory.
story: The summer at RSI was when I learned what real research felt like. Working in MIT's math department on finite field combinatorics, I spent weeks staring at polynomials and feeling completely lost. Then one afternoon, a pattern clicked. I derived a new theorem by combining algebraic and combinatorial arguments in a way nobody had tried before. That moment — from confusion to clarity — taught me that discomfort is where discovery lives.

title: Super-Resolution Model Optimization (Tencent)
description: Optimized ML-based super-resolution models (ECBSR, ESRGAN) to enhance graphics quality in Tencent gaming and streaming products, improving visual quality metrics while reducing inference time.
story: At Tencent, I learned that research means nothing if it can't ship. I was tasked with making super-resolution models faster without sacrificing image quality for gaming and streaming products. Balancing PSNR scores against inference latency on real hardware forced me to think about engineering trade-offs I'd never considered in a classroom. It was my first taste of production ML, and it changed how I think about the gap between a paper and a product.

title: Interactive Spyfall Game with AI
description: Developed a full-stack interactive web application for the social deduction game Spyfall, featuring AI-powered opponents that can ask and answer questions using natural language understanding.
story: Freshman year at MIT, I wanted to build something purely fun. Spyfall — the social deduction party game — seemed perfect. I built a web app where you could play against AI opponents that actually asked clever questions and gave suspicious (but not too suspicious) answers. Integrating NLP into a real-time game taught me about prompt engineering, websockets, and the art of making AI feel human. Friends loved it. The AI was surprisingly good at lying.

title: Time Series Forecasting via Modified Transformer Attention
description: Conducted research on hierarchical revenue forecasting for a medical device company, replacing standard Transformer attention with a modified attention-on-attention mechanism to better capture multi-level temporal dependencies.
story: At the MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab, I wrestled with a problem that sounds simple but isn't: forecasting sales at multiple levels of a product hierarchy simultaneously. Standard Transformers didn't respect the constraint that child forecasts should sum to parent totals. I modified the attention mechanism and designed custom hierarchical loss functions. The result was a model that was not only more accurate but structurally coherent — a lesson that constraints aren't limitations, they're design principles.

title: Gene Information Web Application
description: Developed a full-stack bioinformatics web portal for rapid gene information lookup, aggregating data from Ensembl and MyGene APIs with batch processing and a research-oriented UI.
story: At Biostate AI, I built a gene lookup portal that researchers actually wanted to use. The challenge was stitching together data from multiple bioinformatics APIs (Ensembl, MyGene) that each had their own quirks and rate limits. Batch query processing cut latency dramatically. But the real lesson was in UX — scientists don't want flashy dashboards, they want fast, reliable answers. I redesigned the interface three times before it clicked.

title: Toki (Manufacturing Scheduling & ERP Layer)
description: Designed a dynamic production-line scheduling system for automotive glass manufacturing, modeling machine constraints and urgent-order prioritization with a modular SaaS architecture.
story: Toki taught me that the hardest engineering problems aren't algorithmic — they're human. Building a scheduling system for a real factory meant understanding that operators communicate through Excel, that 'urgent' means different things to different departments, and that the best algorithm is useless if workers won't trust it. I learned to design systems that meet people where they are, not where I wish they were.

title: Dramaverse (Agentic AI Short-Drama Platform)
description: Built an end-to-end agentic workflow for AI-native short-drama creation, integrating specialized agents for scriptwriting, art direction, video synthesis, voice, and sound design into a coherent pipeline.
story: Dramaverse was my most ambitious project — an entire AI film production pipeline. Each agent (scriptwriter, art director, casting, video synthesis, sound design) had to communicate and build on each other's outputs. The hardest problem? Character consistency across scenes. I trained LoRA models on 60+ curated images per character and experimented with ControlNet and depth conditioning. The result isn't perfect, but it proved that AI-native storytelling is possible beyond 'AI slop.' That's the future I want to build.

title: Personal Portfolio Website
description: Designed and developed this personal portfolio website using SvelteKit, featuring dark/light theming, scrollytelling project narratives, GitHub API integration, and responsive design.
story: Meta, I know — but building this website was its own journey. Learning SvelteKit, reactive stores, layout components, and scrollytelling forced me to think about narrative and design in ways I never had before. Every section of this site represents a deliberate choice: what to show, what to hide, and how to tell the story of who I am as an engineer. If you've scrolled this far, I hope the story made sense.
